*County:* Conwy
*Type:* castle

*Wikipedia:* https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dolwyddelan_Castle

*Main Image:* https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/85/Dolwyddelan_Castle%2C_Conwy%2C_Wales_%28cropped%29.jpg/330px-Dolwyddelan_Castle%2C_Conwy%2C_Wales_%28cropped%29.jpg
*Gallery:* 0 images
*Image Source:* wikipedia
*Has Wikipedia:* yes
*Has Google Places:* unknown

*Description:*
Dolwyddelan Castle is a thirteenth-century castle located west of Dolwyddelan in Conwy County Borough, Wales. Tomen Castell, a late twelfth-century tower, is located south-east of the castle.

_Listing 1 of 61_

listing.name: Dolwyddelan Castle
listing.desc: Dolwyddelan Castle is a thirteenth-century castle located west of Dolwyddelan in Conwy County Borough, Wales. Tomen Castell, a late twelfth-century tower, is located south-east of the castle.

imageAttribution.description: Dolwyddelan Castle is a fortress near Conwy, Wales.. Dolwyddelan castle was built by Llywelyn; the old castle nearby may have been his birthplace.

items.text:
🔍 *ENRICH REVIEW*  \[✨ ENRICHED\]

*Name:* Penmon Priory
*Country:* wales
*Region:* snowdonia north
*County:* Anglesey
*Type:* abbey
*Period:* 6th-13th century
*Coords:* 53.305833, -4.056667
*Wikipedia:* https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Penmon_Priory
*Maps:* https://www.google.com/maps/search/Penmon%20Priory/@53.3167,-4.05,15z
*Main Image:* https://img.castlecore.uk/penmon-priory.jpg
*Gallery:* 0 images
*Image Source:* CDN
*Has Wikipedia:* yes
*Has Google Places:* unknown
*Enrich Notes:* update google places link - https://maps.app.goo.gl/UStq5RQbtmmgoTgP6


*Description:*
Penmon is a promontory, village and ecclesiastical parish on the eastern tip of the Isle of Anglesey in Wales, about 3 miles (4.8 km) east of the town of Beaumaris. It is in the community of Llangoed. The name comes from Welsh: pen and Môn, which is the Welsh name for Anglesey. It is the site of a historic monastery and associated 12th-century church. Walls near the well next to the church may be part of the oldest remaining Christian building in Wales. Penmon also has an award-winning beach and the Anglesey Coastal Path follows its shores. Quarries in Penmon have provided stone for many important buildings and structures, including Birmingham Town Hall and the two bridges that cross the Menai Strait. The area is popular with locals and visitors alike for its monuments, tranquillity, bracing air and fine views of Snowdonia to the south across the Menai Strait.

_Listing 2 of 61_

listing.name: Penmon Priory
listing.desc: Penmon is a promontory, village and ecclesiastical parish on the eastern tip of the Isle of Anglesey in Wales, about 3 miles (4.8 km) east of the town of Beaumaris. 🔍 *ENRICH REVIEW*  \[✨ ENRICHED\]

*Name:* Aberconwy House
*Country:* wales
*Region:* snowdonia north
*County:* Conwy
*Type:* historic-house
*Period:* 14th century
*Coords:* 53.2816, -3.8285
*Wikipedia:* https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Aberconwy_House
*Maps:* https://www.google.com/maps/search/Aberconwy%20House/@53.2806,-3.8278,15z
*Main Image:* https://img.castlecore.uk/aberconwy-house.jpg
*Gallery:* 4 images
*Image Source:* CDN
*Has Wikipedia:* yes
*Has Google Places:* unknown
*Enrich Notes:* update google places link - https://maps.app.goo.gl/84opEhcfCWrC1tML9


*Description:*
Aberconwy House, 2 Castle Street, Conwy, Wales is a medieval merchant's house and one of the oldest dateable houses in Wales. Constructed in the 15th century it is, along with Plas Mawr, one of the two surviving merchant's houses within the town. Its historical and architectural importance is reflected in its status as a Grade I listed building. The house is administered by the National Trust.

_Listing 3 of 61_

listing.name: Aberconwy House
listing.desc: Aberconwy House, 2 Castle Street, Conwy, Wales is a medieval merchant's house and one of the oldest dateable houses in Wales. Its historical and architectural importance is reflected in its status as a Grade I listed building. The house is administered by the National Trust.

items.text:
🔍 *ENRICH REVIEW*  \[✨ ENRICHED\]

*Name:* Coity Castle
*Country:* wales
*Region:* south wales
*County:* Bridgend
*Type:* castle
*Period:* 12th century
*Coords:* 51.521944, -3.553056
*Wikipedia:* https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Coity_Castle
*Maps:* https://www.google.com/maps/search/Coity%20Castle/@51.5259,-3.5491,15z
*Main Image:* https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/84/Coity_Castle.jpg/330px-Coity_Castle.jpg
*Gallery:* 0 images
*Image Source:* wikipedia
*Has Wikipedia:* yes
*Has Google Places:* unknown

*Description:*
Coity Castle in Glamorgan, Wales, is a Norman castle built by Sir Payn "the Demon" de Turberville, one of the legendary Twelve Knights of Glamorgan supposed to have conquered Glamorgan under the leadership of Robert FitzHamon, Lord of Gloucester. Now in ruins, it stands in the community of Coity Higher near the town of Bridgend, in the County Borough of Bridgend. Very close to the castle is the battlemented parish Church of St Mary the Virgin, which dates from the 14th century.

_Listing 4 of 61_

listing.name: Coity Castle
listing.desc: Coity Castle in Glamorgan, Wales, is a Norman castle built by Sir Payn "the Demon" de Turberville, one of the legendary Twelve Knights of Glamorgan supposed to have conquered Glamorgan under the leadership of Robert FitzHamon, Lord of Gloucester. Now in ruins, it stands in the community of Coity Higher near the town of Bridgend, in the County Borough of Bridgend. Very close to the castle is the battlemented parish Church of St Mary the Virgin, which dates from the 14th century.

imageAttribution.description: Coity Castle is a Norman castle in the community of Coity Higher near the town of Bridgend, in the County Borough of Bridgend in South Wales.

items.text:
🔍 *ENRICH REVIEW*  \[✨ ENRICHED\]

*Name:* Cilgerran Castle
*Country:* wales
*Region:* west wales
*County:* Pembrokeshire
*Type:* castle
*Period:* 13th century
*Coords:* 52.05722222, -4.63416667
*Wikipedia:* https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cilgerran_Castle
*Maps:* https://www.google.com/maps/search/Cilgerran%20Castle/@52.0838,-4.6846,15z
*Main Image:* https://lh3.googleusercontent.com/place-photos/AJRVUZNmLv3Wmea5e7yv7CemdiXmfYkS2CaeH3SLUiUO2jBmS7ihllQVvN9cbjtpb27oO4jiL4-C3nwqr1o40jHCCdkQ45MiTb69UGbIIRX5UT403x56Kh34BvcKareQs5zdbtRrBBQ8CeICegGEWRE=s4800-w1200
*Gallery:* 0 images
*Image Source:* google
*Has Wikipedia:* unknown
*Has Google Places:* unknown

*Description:*
Cilgerran Castle is a 13th-century ruined castle located in Cilgerran, Pembrokeshire, Wales, near Cardigan. The first castle on the site was thought to have been built by Gerald of Windsor around 1110–1115, and it changed hands several times over the following century between English and Welsh forces. In the hands of William Marshal, 2nd Earl of Pembroke, the construction of the stone castle began after 1223.

_Listing 5 of 61_

🔍 *ENRICH REVIEW*  \[🔧 STILL IN PROGRESS\]

*Name:* Garn Goch
*Country:* wales
*Region:* west wales
*County:* Carmarthenshire
*Type:* hillfort
*Period:* Iron Age (c.800-100 BC)
*Coords:* 51.902, -3.905
*Wikipedia:* https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Y_Garn_Goch
*Maps:* https://www.google.com/maps/search/Garn%20Goch/@51.8833,-3.7833,15z
*Main Image:* https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/19/Y_Garn_Coch_fort.JPG/330px-Y_Garn_Coch_fort.JPG
*Gallery:* 0 images
*Image Source:* CDN
*Has Wikipedia:* yes
*Has Google Places:* unknown
*Enrich Notes:* needs more research 

*Description:*
Y Garn Goch is a hill in the Brecon Beacons National Park in the east of Carmarthenshire, Wales. The name means the 'red cairn'. It lies near the village of Bethlehem, three miles southwest of Llangadog and four miles east of Llandeilo on the southern side of the broad Towy Valley. It is also commonly known as either Garn Goch or Carn Goch. Current owners and land managers are the Bannau Brycheiniog National Park Authority.

_Listing 6 of 61_

It is also commonly known as either Garn Goch or Carn Goch. Current owners and land managers are the Bannau Brycheiniog National Park Authority.

*Description:*
Castle Roche is a Norman castle located some 10 km north-west of Dundalk, County Louth, Ireland. It was the seat of the de Verdun family, who built the castle in 1236.

_Listing 7 of 61_
